core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing jaboticaba, the Brazilian tree grape, are the same: a warm, frost-free spot, an acid soil that never dries out, the patience to wait a few years for fruit, and the reward of sweet, grape-like berries that erupt straight from the trunk. The state sections below cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Most jaboticabas sold in Australia are the common or Sabara type (Plinia cauliflora), a small-leaved tree carrying small, thin-skinned, very sweet black fruit that is prized for being precocious and heavily productive. Large-leaved selections such as Grimal carry bigger, thicker-skinned fruit on a slower tree. There are also red-skinned types (sold as Scarlet or Escarlate), a rare yellow or white form (often sold as Plinia aureana), and compact dwarf selections that suit pots and small gardens. They are all closely related cauliflorous members of the myrtle family, kin to the grumichama and the guava (see our grumichama and guava guides). For a first tree the common Sabara type is the safe, reliable choice.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree or two?
sections.body: Jaboticaba is self-fertile, so a single tree will fruit on its own; you do not need a second tree or a particular pollinating partner. The sweetly perfumed white flowers are worked by bees, and bee activity in the garden lifts fruit set, but it is not essential. If you have room for only one tree, you will still get a crop.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Jaboticaba likes a warm, sheltered position in full sun, though it tolerates part shade and in fact ripens much of its fruit in the shade of its own canopy. Give it deep, rich, moist but free-draining soil. It is not tolerant of salty or waterlogged ground, and because it is shallow-rooted it must be kept well mulched and must never be allowed to dry out. The big requirement is acidity: jaboticaba wants a soil of about pH 5.5 to 6.5 and struggles on alkaline or limy ground, where the leaves yellow with iron deficiency. It is slow-growing, especially for the first few years, so pick its spot for the long term (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Steady moisture is the heart of growing jaboticaba well. It is shallow-rooted, so water it year round, never let it dry out, and keep a thick organic mulch over the root zone to hold moisture and feed the surface roots. It is a slow grower that responds to regular feeding through the warm season: University of Florida home-garden guidance suggests a balanced, slightly nitrogen-led slow-release fertiliser (a 4-3-4 NPK blend with added calcium, magnesium and sulfur) about three times a year, and light monthly feeds will push along the slow growth of a young tree. The one thing jaboticaba will not forgive is alkaline soil. It prefers an acid soil of about pH 5.5 to 6.5, and on limy or sandy alkaline ground it yellows with iron deficiency, so use an acidic (azalea and camellia) fertiliser, keep the mulch up, and correct any yellowing with iron and trace-element sprays.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Jaboticaba is cauliflorous, which is its party trick: the flowers, and then the fruit, form in dense clusters directly on the trunk, limbs and bare branches rather than out among the leaves. A wave of perfumed blossom is followed by ripe fruit in only about a month (roughly 30 to 45 days), and a happy, well-watered tree will flower and crop in several flushes a year, commonly two or three in the subtropics and up to four or five in warm tropical districts. The grape-sized berries ripen from green through wine-red to glossy black and are picked when fully black and slightly soft. They will not keep: fresh jaboticaba lasts only a day or two at room temperature and a few days refrigerated before it begins to ferment, which is why it is almost never sold in shops and is grown as a backyard and local-market fruit.

sections.heading: Eating and storing
sections.body: Jaboticaba is best eaten fresh and straight off the tree. Squeeze a berry so the tough skin splits and the soft, translucent, grape-like pulp slips into your mouth, then spit out the one to four small seeds. The skin is edible but high in tannin, so most people spit it out, and it is best not eaten in quantity day after day. A glut is turned into jelly, jam, juice, wine and liqueur, and the deep-coloured skins are often used to give those preserves their rich colour. Because the fresh fruit will not store or travel, preserving is the usual way to keep a heavy crop.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Jaboticaba is unusual among fruit trees in coming more or less true from seed: the seeds are polyembryonic, so a seedling is usually close to its parent. The catch is speed. A seedling is famously slow and can take anywhere from eight to fifteen years to bear its first fruit, so a cheap seedling is a gift to your future self, not a quick crop. A grafted, air-layered or cutting-grown tree from a selected type fruits far sooner, often within about three to five years, which is what an advanced or grafted plant in the table above is really buying you. Decide whether to pay more for a head start or to plant a seedling and wait, and either way pick a named type (Sabara, Grimal, red or yellow) so you know what you are getting.

faqs.q: Can you grow a jaboticaba in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, and it suits one well. Jaboticaba is naturally slow and compact, dwarf selections especially so, which makes it a good long-term tub plant for a courtyard or balcony. Use a large pot and a free-draining acidic mix, never let it dry out, feed regularly because containers leach nutrients, and in cold areas move the pot under cover for winter.

faqs.q: How big does a jaboticaba tree get, and can you keep it small?
faqs.a: Left for decades it can slowly reach eight to twelve metres, but it is so slow and takes pruning so well that it is easily held to two or three metres or grown as a dense evergreen hedge. Prune it to shape after a flush of fruit; the cauliflorous habit means you can shorten branches without losing next season's crop.

faqs.q: What does a jaboticaba taste like?
faqs.a: Like a sweet, aromatic grape with a hint of lychee. You bite or squeeze the thick skin, and a soft, jelly-like, translucent white pulp bursts out, sweet and faintly tangy around the seeds. Fully ripe black fruit is the sweetest; the skin itself is tannic and is usually spat out.

faqs.q: Is jaboticaba an easy tree to grow?
faqs.a: Once it is established in the right spot it is one of the most trouble-free fruit trees you can plant: hardy, long-lived, largely ignored by fruit fly, and rarely needing spraying. The one demand is patience, because it grows and comes into bearing slowly, so the hard part is the wait, not the care.

WA.intro: Growing jaboticaba in Western Australia. The Brazilian tree grape suits the frost-light Perth coast, where it grows well and, thanks to its tough skin, mostly escapes the fruit fly that plagues other backyard fruit.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Jaboticaba likes warmth without the worst of the heat, which suits much of coastal WA. Around Perth the mild Mediterranean climate and only light coastal frosts let it grow well, and a long-standing WA view is that it has real potential here as both a home-garden and a small commercial tree. Mature trees shrug off brief light frosts, but young plants are tender, and the hot, dry inland needs steady summer water to keep the shallow roots cool and moist.

sections.heading: Where jaboticaba grows in WA
sections.body: The reliable ground is the frost-free coast. Perth gardens and the wider Swan Coastal Plain grow jaboticaba in sheltered, sunny spots with summer water. The warmer, drier Gascoyne around Carnarvon ripens a wide range of subtropical fruit and suits it too, while the frost hollows of the Perth Hills and the cold inland are marginal for young trees.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: With summer water a WA tree will flower and fruit in more than one flush. A Perth grower has reported getting at least three crops in a single year from one established tree. Expect the main flushes through the warm months, each lot of fruit ripening about a month after its wave of blossom, and pick the berries when they are fully black and give slightly.

sections.heading: Fruit fly and jaboticaba in WA
sections.body: This is jaboticaba's WA party trick. The Mediterranean fruit fly is established right across WA and ruins much of the state's backyard stone and pome fruit, but jaboticaba's skin is so thick that the fly mostly cannot reach the flesh, and growers rate it among the few genuinely fruit-fly-resistant trees you can plant. That makes it an unusually low-spray choice for a Perth garden, though no fruit is completely immune in a bad year.

sections.heading: Myrtle rust and getting a tree to WA
sections.body: Jaboticaba belongs to the myrtle family, the group myrtle rust attacks, but WA is still almost free of that disease (the only detection was a remote site in the far north in 2022), which is good news for growers here. The plant itself is permitted entry to WA, yet because it is a myrtle-family plant, live nursery stock still needs treatment and certification under WA's myrtle-rust import rules, so several eastern nurseries will not post it across the border. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

faqs.q: Which jaboticaba should I plant in Perth?
faqs.a: For a first tree the common Sabara (small-leaf) type is the most reliable and productive. Any jaboticaba will grow on the frost-free coast, so beyond that choose by fruit: a large-leaved Grimal for a bigger berry, or a red or yellow type for novelty. Whatever you pick, keep the water up through the dry summer.

faqs.q: Will jaboticaba grow in Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes, but Perth's coastal sand is often alkaline and low in iron, which jaboticaba dislikes, so the leaves can yellow. Dig in plenty of compost, mulch thickly, use an acidic (azalea) fertiliser to bring the pH down, and treat any yellowing with an iron and trace-element spray.

QLD.intro: Growing jaboticaba in Queensland. Warm, humid Queensland is prime jaboticaba country, from the subtropical south east up to the tropical far north, and the tree was one of the first rare fruits trialled as a local crop here.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Jaboticaba thrives in Queensland's warmth. It is at its best in the humid subtropical south east, where it grows into an easy, productive tree, while in the tropical far north it fruits even more often through the year, though the individual crops tend to be smaller. The main trade-off for all that warmth and humidity is disease pressure from myrtle rust.

sections.heading: Where jaboticaba grows in QLD
sections.body: The tree has a long Queensland history. One of Australia's first jaboticaba orchards was planted in the wallum country around Cootharaba, in the Sunshine Coast hinterland north of Noosa, and it is still grown through the Sunshine Coast and Wide Bay districts and around Gympie. Further north it crops on the Atherton Tableland behind Cairns. In a suburban yard it makes an ornamental specimen or a productive evergreen hedge.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: In the warm subtropics a jaboticaba flushes and fruits more than once a year, and in the tropical north it can crop for much of the year in repeated waves. Each crop ripens about a month after flowering. Pick the berries when they are fully black and yield to a gentle press, and eat or preserve them quickly before they ferment.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Myrtle rust is the disease to know. It thrives on the humid Queensland coast and infects jaboticaba and its myrtle-family relatives, so space and prune trees for airflow and watch the soft new growth. Queensland fruit fly is endemic here, but jaboticaba's thick skin makes it largely fruit-fly resistant, a rare easy-care trait among Queensland fruit. Otherwise the worst you are likely to see is a little woolly aphid on the spring growth and birds after the ripe fruit.

faqs.a: Almost any does well in the Queensland warmth, so choose by what you want on the plate: the common Sabara type for reliable, heavy crops of small sweet fruit, a large-leaved Grimal for bigger berries, or a red or yellow selection for something unusual. All cope with the humidity, and all share the same myrtle-rust watch.

faqs.q: Does jaboticaba make a good hedge in Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes. It is naturally dense and slow, takes pruning well, and is often planted as a productive evergreen hedge or a feature specimen in south-east Queensland gardens. A single plant becomes a feature; a row makes a screen that also fruits along its stems, which is a fair reward for the wait.

NSW.intro: Growing jaboticaba in New South Wales. The subtropical Northern Rivers is the heart of jaboticaba growing in Australia, but the tree's cold-hardiness lets it reach well down the coast, even into sheltered Sydney gardens.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Jaboticaba is well suited to coastal NSW. The warm, humid Northern Rivers grows it about as easily as it grows avocados, and because mature trees take a light frost the tree also succeeds further south and inland than most subtropical fruit, down to warm, sheltered pockets of the Sydney basin. Growth slows over winter, but established coastal trees keep cropping through it.

sections.heading: Where jaboticaba grows in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers around the Tweed, Lismore and Alstonville is the established district, and a grafted tree at the old Alstonville research station fruited just three and a half years after planting out. The tree carries on down the mid-north coast around Coffs Harbour, and keen gardeners fruit it in frost-free corners of the Sydney basin, more slowly than on the warm north coast.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the warm north coast a jaboticaba gives two or three crops a year, against as many as five in the tropics, each ripening about a month after flowering. The main flushes come in the warmer half of the year, and even in midwinter the established coastal trees often hold some fruit. Pick the berries fully black and use them within a couple of days.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Myrtle rust matters most here. It was first found in Australia on the NSW central coast in 2010, and it infects jaboticaba and the whole myrtle family, hitting hardest in the humid coastal districts where the tree grows best, so prune for airflow and watch soft new growth. Queensland fruit fly is native to north-eastern NSW, but jaboticaba's thick skin makes it largely fruit-fly resistant. Birds are the other competitor for the ripe fruit.

faqs.q: Can you grow jaboticaba in Sydney?
faqs.a: Yes, more readily than many subtropical fruits, because mature jaboticaba takes a light frost. Give it a warm, sheltered, north-facing, frost-free spot, protect young plants through their first winters, and accept that it will grow and fruit more slowly than it does on the warm north coast.

faqs.q: Which jaboticaba variety suits the NSW north coast?
faqs.a: The warm north coast grows them all, so pick by fruit. The common Sabara type is the dependable heavy cropper of small sweet berries, a large-leaved Grimal gives bigger fruit, and red or yellow selections are fun rarities. Give any of them a sunny, frost-free spot and steady moisture.

VIC.intro: Growing jaboticaba in Victoria. Victoria is cool for a Brazilian fruit, but jaboticaba is the frost-hardiest of the bunch, so with a warm microclimate or a pot it is one of the more achievable rare fruits for a Melbourne garden.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Most subtropical fruit will not take a Victorian winter, but jaboticaba is unusually cold-tolerant: mature trees handle light frosts, and it will grow in temperate districts with cool winters, just very slowly. In practice that means a warm, sheltered, north-facing spot in and around Melbourne, or a large pot you can move, rather than an exposed, frost-prone garden.

sections.heading: Where jaboticaba grows in VIC
sections.body: The best chances are the mild microclimates: warm, north-facing corners of Melbourne, the bayside suburbs and the Mornington Peninsula, and the milder coastal pockets of Gippsland. Many Victorian growers sidestep the question entirely and keep jaboticaba in a large tub that spends winter under cover. Either way, expect slow growth and a long wait for the first fruit.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Where it fruits at all, a Victorian jaboticaba crops mainly in the warmer months, and in a cool season the late fruit may not finish ripening before the cold sets in. There are fewer flushes than on the warm coast. Let the berries colour right through to glossy black on the tree, since they do not ripen well once picked.

sections.heading: Frost, pests and pots in VIC
sections.body: Cold, not pests, is the limiting factor in Victoria. Protect young plants from frost with cloth and a warm wall through their first winters, while mature trees are tougher. Myrtle rust is present in Victoria and can affect jaboticaba as a myrtle-family plant, and Queensland fruit fly is established in the state's fruit-growing north, though the tree's thick skin makes it largely fruit-fly resistant. A frost-hardier myrtle relative to fall back on, if jaboticaba proves too slow, is the feijoa (see our feijoa guide).

faqs.q: How do I protect a jaboticaba from frost in Victoria?
faqs.a: Young plants are the vulnerable ones. Grow the tree in the warmest, most sheltered, north-facing spot you have, or in a pot you can move under cover, wrap it in frost cloth on cold nights through its first winters, and mulch the roots well. Ease off water in the cold so it is not pushing tender new growth. Established trees handle a light frost.

faqs.q: How long will a jaboticaba take to fruit in Victoria?
faqs.a: Longer than anywhere warmer. The cool climate slows an already slow tree, so a seedling could take well over a decade to bear. To have any hope of fruit in a reasonable time, start with an advanced, grafted or cutting-grown plant rather than a seedling, and treat the tree as a long-term project.
